In France, voting has traditionally been a low-tech experience: the voter goes into a small booth, and places a pre-printed piece of paper indicating the chosen candidate into an envelope.
Once the voter's identity has been verified by an official, the voter drops the envelope into a ballot box and signs the voter roll.

When a small group of organisms (a small population) becomes separated from the main population they came from (for example, they cross a mountain range or river, or they end up on a new island so that it is not easy to go back), they often find themselves in a different environment than the one they were in before.

This new environment has different resources and different competitors, so the new population will need different characteristics or adaptations to be a strong competitor than the ones they had before.
The original population has not changed at all, and still needs the same adaptations as before.
Over time, as the new population begins to adapt to the new environment, it begins to become more and more different from the other population.
After thousands, or even millions of years, the two populations would eventually look so different that they could no longer be called the same species.
We call this process speciation, which means the formation of new species. Speciation is an inevitable consequence of evolution, and an important part of it.

Technological determinism is a term that encompasses a wide variety of ideas in practice, including technological push, technological necessity, and a strict definition that human destiny is driven by the underlying logic related to scientific laws and their manifestation in technology.

Most interpretations of technological determinism include both of these general ideas: that the development of technology itself is to a large extent beyond cultural or political influence, and that technology in turn has an inherent "effect" on society that is not conditioned by social conditions.
For example, one might say that the car necessarily leads to the development of roads.
However, a national network of roads is only economically feasible for a small number of cars, so new methods of production are developed to lower the cost of ownership of the car.
The higher number of cars in use also leads to a higher number of road accidents, which in turn drives the invention of new medical technologies to repair the injuries.

Although the horse-drawn railways were nothing more than parallel planks of wood, they allowed the horses to pull more and faster than on the somewhat rough roads of the time.
Ties were introduced early on to hold the rails in place, but it was gradually realized that the rails would be more effective if they were made of iron.
This became common practice, but the iron plates wore down the wooden wheels of the carriages.
Eventually, the wooden wheels were replaced by iron wheels. In 1767, the first all-iron track was introduced.
The first known form of transportation was walking. Two million years ago, humans started walking upright with the appearance of Homo Erectus, meaning upright man.
Their ancestors, the australopithecines, did not have the habit of walking upright.
Bipedal specialisation has been found in Australopithecus fossils dating from 4.2 to 3.9 million years ago, although Sahelanthropus may have walked on two legs as early as 7 million years ago.

In many cases this is similar to symptomatic treatment. But if we don't just want a temporary solution, we should find the root of the problem and eradicate it.
It is clear that the world has changed greatly due to human scientific and technological advances, and that the problems have been made worse by overpopulation and the human lifestyle of excess.

The handwritten draft, signed by President John Hancock and Secretary Charles Thomson, was approved by the Continental Congress on July 4th and then sent to the John Dunlap printing press, a few blocks away.
Between 150 and 200 were printed overnight, now known as the "Dunlap broadsides".
On July 8, John Nixon read the document publicly for the first time in the yard of the Independence Hall.
One was sent to George Washington on July 6, and he read it to his troops in New York on July 9. A copy arrived in London on August 10.
The 25 surviving Dunlap broadsides are the earliest extant versions of the document. The handwritten original has not survived.
Many paleontologists believe that a group of dinosaurs survived and still live today, which we call birds.
Many people do not think of them as dinosaurs because they have feathers and can fly.
But birds still have a lot in common with dinosaurs.
They have scales and claws on their feet, lay eggs, and walk on two legs like a Tyrannosaurus.
Today, almost all computers in use are based on the processing of information encoded in binary.
A binary number can only be one of two values, either 0 or 1, and such numbers are called binary digits, or bits, in computer terminology.
Ingestion poisoning may not be immediately evident, and symptoms such as vomiting are so common that a diagnosis cannot be made immediately.
The most telling sign of ingestion may be an open container of a drug or household toxic chemical at the scene.
Check the label on the poison for specific first aid instructions.
The entomologists have formally named this group of insects "bugs".
The term is derived from familiarity with the bed bug, a highly adapted insect that parasitizes humans.
Both assassin bugs and bed bugs are nidicolous insects, adapted to living in the nest or habitation of their hosts.
In the United States, there are about 400,000 cases of multiple sclerosis (MS), making it the most common neurological disease of middle-aged and young adults.
Multiple sclerosis is a disease that affects the central nervous system, which is made up of the brain, spinal cord, and optic nerves.
Research has found that women are twice as likely to develop MS as men.
A couple may decide not to have children for their own good or for the good of their children.
These couples may make an adoption plan for their child.
In an adoption, the birth parents terminate their rights as parents so that another couple can raise the child.

An experiment is a test used to eliminate one or more possible hypotheses, however, experiments are not the only method. Questions and observations can also guide scientific research.

Many of the physiological and behavioural rhythms observed by researchers are often highly dependent on the presence and the endogenous cycles produced by the circadian clock.
Circadian rhythms are not simply a reaction to external periodic signals. These rhythms have been recorded in most living organisms, including bacteria, fungi, plants, and animals.
Biological clocks are self-sustaining oscillators that will continue to run for a period of free-running cycles even in the absence of external cues.
The Hershey and Chase experiment was one of the major pieces of evidence that DNA is the genetic material.
Hershey and Chase used bacteriophages, or viruses, to implant their own DNA into bacteria.
They did two experiments, one where they labeled the phage's DNA with radioactive phosphorus, and one where they labeled the phage's protein with radioactive sulfur.
Mutations can have a wide variety of different effects, depending on the type of mutation, the importance of the genetic material affected, and whether the affected cell is a germ cell.
Only mutations in germ cells can be passed on to children, while mutations in other cells can lead to cell death or cancer.
Nature-based tourism attracts visitors who are interested in natural areas and come to see the natural scenery, including wildlife.
On-site activities include hunting, fishing, photography, bird watching, visiting parks, and studying ecosystems.
For example, visiting, photographing and learning about orangutans in Borneo.
Every morning, people drive out of small towns to work, passing by people who work where they are leaving from.
In this dynamic traffic shuttle, everyone is connected to and supports the car-based transportation system in some way.
Today, science shows that this large carbon economy is no longer keeping the biosphere in the stable state that has supported human evolution for the past two million years.
Everyone is part of society and uses the transportation system. Almost everyone complains about the transportation system.
In developed countries, you rarely hear complaints of a similar order about water quality or bridges collapsing.
Why do transportation systems generate such complaints? Why do they fail every day? Are transportation engineers incompetent? Or is there something more fundamental going on?
Traffic flow studies the movement of drivers and vehicles between two points, and the interactions between them.
Unfortunately, traffic flow is difficult to study because driver behavior cannot be predicted with 100% certainty.
Fortunately, driver behavior tends to fall within a reasonable range of consistency; therefore, traffic flow tends to have a reasonable consistency that can be roughly represented mathematically.
To better represent traffic flow, relationships have been established between three main characteristics: (1) flow, (2) density, and (3) speed.
These relationships are helpful in planning, designing, and operating road facilities.
Insects were the first animals to fly in the air. The ability to fly made it easier for them to escape from enemies and more efficient to find food and mates.
Most insects have the advantage of being able to fold their wings back along their body.
This gives them more small places to hide from predators.
Today, the only insects that cannot fold their wings backwards are dragonflies and mayflies.
Thousands of years ago, a man named Aristarchus said that the solar system was moving around the sun.
Some people thought he was right, but many others thought the opposite; that the solar system was moving around the Earth, and so was the Sun (and even other stars).
This seems reasonable, because the Earth doesn't feel like it's moving, does it?
The Amazon River is the second longest and the largest river in the world. It carries more than eight times the volume of water than the second largest river.
The Amazon is also the widest river on earth, with some sections being 6 miles wide.
The Amazon carries 20% of the world's river flow into the sea.
The main stem of the Amazon River is 6,387 km (3,980 miles) long. The water comes from thousands of smaller tributaries.

The Ancient Egyptians of the New Kingdom marveled at the monuments of their forebears, which were over a thousand years old.
Vatican City has a population of about 800 people. It is the smallest independent country in the world and the country with the smallest population.
Vatican City uses Italian for its laws and official communications.
Italian is also the language most people working in the country use on a daily basis, while Latin is often used in religious ceremonies.
All citizens of Vatican City are Roman Catholics.

This is more like the four states of matter (in the same order): solid, liquid, gas, and plasma, though he also theorized that they would transform into new substances that make up what we see.
Alloys are basically a mixture of two or more metals. Don't forget that there are many elements on the Periodic Table.
Elements such as calcium and potassium are metals, as are elements such as silver and gold, of course.
There are also alloys with small amounts of non-metallic elements (such as carbon).

Unfortunately, as new ways of writing have emerged, the pencil has become less important and less used.

It's a bit like pushing a heavy cart up a hill. Splitting the nucleus again then releases some of that energy.
Some atoms have unstable nuclei, which means they are likely to split with little or no provocation.
The Moon's surface is made of rock and dust. The Moon's outer layer is called the crust.
The crust is about 70 km thick on the near side and about 100 km thick on the far side.
It is thinner under the maria and thicker under the highlands.
Because the crust is thinner, there may be more maria on the near side, where lava can more easily rise to the surface.
The core of content theories is to find what motivates or interests people.
These theories suggest that certain needs and/or desires are internalized as people mature into adulthood.
These theories explore why certain people want to do what they do, and what in their environment will cause them to do or not do certain things.
Two popular content theories are Maslow's hierarchy of needs and Herzberg's two-factor theory.
Generally, two behaviors emerge when managers begin to lead former peers. In one case, they strive to maintain a "close relationship" with their team members.
Managers of this type have difficulty making unpopular decisions, and have difficulty with discipline, performance evaluations, assigning responsibility, and holding people accountable.
At the other extreme, the person becomes a different person than he or she was before, feeling that he or she must change everything the team has done and take credit for it.
After all, the leader is ultimately responsible for the success or failure of the team.
This behavior often leads to conflict between the leader and the other members of the team.
Virtual teams need to adhere to the same quality standards as traditional teams, but with some subtle differences.
Virtual team members often serve as the point of contact for their corresponding onsite team.
They often have more autonomy than traditional team members, as their teams may schedule meetings at times that are not understood by local management in different time zones.
The existence of a real "invisible team" (Larson and LaFasto, 1989, p109) is also a unique component of a virtual team.
The "invisible team" is the management team to which each member reports. It sets the standards for all members.
Why would an organization want to spend the time to create a learning organization? One of the goals of putting the concept of organizational learning into practice is innovation.
Creativity and innovation occur when all available resources are effectively used between the functional areas of an organization.
Therefore, as an organization works together to overcome obstacles, it often leads to new innovative processes to meet the needs of the customer.
Before an organization can innovate, leaders must create a culture of innovation, share knowledge, and engage in organized learning.
The Angel (2006) explains the Continuum approach as a way to help organizations reach higher levels of performance.
Neurobiological data provides physical evidence for theoretical approaches to the study of cognition. It narrows the scope of research and makes it more precise.
Correlations between brain pathology and behavior can support the scientist's research.
We have known for a long time that different types of brain damage, trauma, lesions and tumors can affect behavior and cause certain mental functions to change.
The rise of new technologies has allowed us to see and study the structure and processes of the brain in ways that have never been seen before.
This gives us a wealth of information and material to build simulation models that help us understand the processes of how the brain works.

Artificial intelligence has a strong science fiction connotation, but it is actually a very important branch of computer science that deals with machine behavior, learning, and intelligent adaptation.
AI research involves creating machines to automate tasks that require intelligent behavior.
Examples include control, planning and scheduling, the ability to respond to customer diagnosis and questions, and handwriting recognition, voice and facial recognition.
All of these have become separate disciplines, focusing on providing solutions to real life problems.
AI systems are now often used in the fields of economics, medicine, engineering, and the military, and some home computer and video game software applications have AI systems built in.
Field trips are an important part of any educational program. Often teachers are eager to take students to places that cannot be reached by bus.
Virtual field trips are a high-tech solution. Students can view museum artifacts, visit aquariums, or appreciate fine art in the classroom.
Sharing field trips virtually is also a good way to review the trip and share experiences with future classes.

For example, students at Bennet School in North Carolina design a website each year about their trip to the state capital. The website is redesigned each year, but the older versions are kept online as a scrapbook.
Blogs can also help students improve their writing skills. Although students often start blogs with poor grammar and spelling, the presence of an audience often changes that.
Students tend to be the most critical readers, so the bloggers begin to improve their writing to avoid criticism.
In addition, blogging "forces students to be more aware of the world around them". In order to satisfy the audience's interest, students are inspired to be smart and interesting.
Blogging is a tool that can foster collaboration and encourage students to extend their learning beyond the traditional classroom.

The appropriate use of blogs "can make students better at analysis and speculation; by actively responding to online materials, students can find their place in the context of others' writing and can offer their own perspectives on particular issues" (Oravec, 2002).
Ottawa, Canada's capital, is a charming bilingual city with a good selection of art galleries and museums that showcase Canada's past and present.
Further south are the Niagara Falls, and to the north are the Muskoka and the unspoiled natural beauty across the river.
All of this and more has made Ontario the province that most outsiders think of as Canada.
The vast areas further north are sparsely populated, with some areas being uninhabited wilderness.
A surprising population comparison: there are more African Americans living in the United States than there are Canadian citizens.
The East African islands are located in the Indian Ocean off the east coast of Africa.
Madagascar is by far the largest island and is a continent in itself for wildlife.
The smaller islands are mostly independent countries or French territories, known for their luxury beach resorts.
The Arabs also brought Islam to the land, which became dominant in the Comoros and Mayotte.
European influence and colonialism began in the 15th century with the discovery by the Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama of a route from Europe to Cape Town in India.

The modern city of Casablanca was founded by Berber fishermen in the 10th century BC and was used as a strategic port by the Phoenicians, Romans and Merenids, under the name Anfa.
The Portuguese destroyed and rebuilt the city, naming it Casa Branca, and abandoned it after the earthquake of 1755.
The Moroccan Sultan rebuilt the city as Daru l-Badya. Spanish merchants established a trading base there and named it Casablanca.
Casablanca is one of the most boring places to shop in all of Morocco.
Near the Medina, there are shops everywhere selling Moroccan traditional goods such as tagines, pottery, leather goods, hookahs and various knick-knacks, but they are all tourist-oriented.
Goma is a tourist town in the Democratic Republic of Congo, located in the far east of Congo, near Rwanda.
In 2002, Goma was destroyed by lava from the Nyiragongo volcano, which buried most of the town's streets, especially in the downtown area.
While Goma is relatively safe, any travel outside Goma should be researched in advance to see if the fighting in North Kivu has eased.
The city is also the base for climbing Nyiragongo volcano and for the cheapest mountain gorilla tracking in Africa.
You can take a boda-boda (rented motorcycle) around Goma. The normal (local) price for a short ride is 500 Congolese francs.
And because of its relative remoteness, "Timbuktu" is used as a metaphor for a distant exotic place.
Today, Timbuktu is a poor town, although its reputation makes it a tourist destination and it has an airport.
In 1990, it was listed as a World Heritage Site in danger due to the threat of the desert.
It was a major stop on Henry Louis Gates' PBS special, Wonders of the African World.
The city stands in stark contrast to the rest of the country, as it is more Arab than African.
Kruger National Park (KNP) is located in the north-eastern part of South Africa, bordering Mozambique to the east, Zimbabwe to the north and the Crocodile River to the south.
The park covers 19,500 km² and is divided into 14 different ecozones, supporting different wildlife.
It is one of the major attractions of South Africa and is considered the crown jewel of the South African National Parks (SANParks).
Like all South African national parks, there is a daily conservation fee and an entrance fee to the park.
There are also advantages to buying a Wild Card, which gives you access to some or all of the South African parks.
Hong Kong Island is synonymous with Hong Kong and is the focus of many visitors.
The many buildings that make up the Hong Kong skyline are like a glittering bar chart against the waters of Victoria Harbour.
For the best views of Hong Kong, leave the island and head to the Kowloon waterfront on the other side of the harbour.
Most of the urban development on Hong Kong Island is concentrated on reclaimed land on the north shore.
This is where the British colonizers settled, so if you are looking for evidence of the territory's colonial history, this is a good place to start.
The Sundarbans is the world's largest coastal belt of mangroves, stretching 80 km (50 miles) from the coast into the interior of Bangladesh and India.
The Sundarbans have been declared a World Heritage Site by UNESCO. The part of the forest in India is called the Sundarbans National Park.
However, these forests are not just mangrove swamps, but also contain the remains of some of the great jungles that once covered the Ganges Plain.
Sundarbans covers an area of 3850 km2, of which about one third is water/swamp area.
Since 1966, Sundarbans has been a wildlife reserve. It is estimated that there are currently 400 Royal Bengal Tigers and about 30,000 spotted deer living in the area.

Given that inter-district buses are often full, it is recommended to buy tickets a few days in advance.
Most areas are served by comfortable, sturdy Japanese Coaster minibuses.
Shared taxis are a fast and comfortable way to get to nearby places such as Paro (Nu 150) and Punakha (Nu 200).
The Oyapock Bridge is a cable-stayed bridge that crosses the Oyapock River, connecting the Brazilian city of Oiapoque with Saint-Georges de l'Oyapock in French Guiana.
The two towers are 83 m high, the length of the bridge is 378 m, and there are two lanes 3.50 m wide.
The vertical clearance under the bridge is 15 m. The project was completed in August 2011, but it did not start to carry traffic until March 2017.
The bridge is expected to be fully operational by September 2017, when Brazilian customs checkpoints are also expected to be completed.
The Guaraní were the most important indigenous group living in the area of present-day eastern Paraguay, living a semi-nomadic hunter-gatherer lifestyle and practicing subsistence agriculture.
The Chaco region was home to other indigenous tribes such as the Guaycurú and Payaguá, who lived by hunting, gathering and fishing.
Paraguay in the 16th century was known as the "Great Indian Province" and was the result of the encounter between Spanish conquerors and the indigenous groups.
The Spanish began a three-century long colonial period.
Since the founding of Asunción in 1537, Paraguay has managed to retain much of its indigenous character and identity.
Argentina is known for having one of the best polo teams and players in the world.
The largest tournament of the year takes place in December at the polo grounds in Las Cañitas.
Smaller tournaments and matches can also be seen on other days of the year.
For tournament news and how to buy tickets to polo matches, check the Argentine Polo Association website.
The official currency of the Falkland Islands is the Falkland Pound, which is pegged to the British Pound.
Currency can be exchanged at the only bank on the islands, located in Stanley, opposite the FIC West store.
Pounds sterling are generally accepted everywhere on the island, and credit cards and US dollars are often accepted in Stanley.
On the outlying islands credit cards may not be accepted, although British and American currency will be; check with the owner in advance to confirm what payment methods are accepted.
It is almost impossible to exchange Falkland Islands currency outside the islands, so exchange it before leaving.
As Montevideo is south of the equator, it is summer there when it is winter in the northern hemisphere, and vice versa.
Montevideo is in the subtropics; in summer months temperatures often exceed 30°C.
Winters can be colder than they appear: temperatures rarely drop below zero, but the combination of wind and humidity makes it feel colder than the thermometer indicates.
There are no specific "rainy" and "dry" seasons: the amount of rain is roughly the same all year round.
While many of the animals in the park are accustomed to seeing humans, they are still wild animals and should not be fed or disturbed.
Park officials recommend keeping at least 100 yards/meters away from bears and wolves and 25 yards/meters away from all other wildlife!
No matter how docile they may appear, bison, moose, elk, bears, and almost all large animals can attack.
Each year, dozens of visitors are injured by not keeping a proper distance. These animals are huge, wild, and potentially dangerous, so give them some space.
Also, be aware that smells attract bears and other wildlife, so avoid carrying or cooking smelly foods and keep your camp clean.
Apia is the capital of Samoa. The town is located on the island of Upolu and has a population of less than 40,000.
Apia was founded in the 1850s and has been the capital of Samoa since 1959.

With two harbours, Auckland has many beaches. The most popular are spread across three areas.
The North Shore Beaches (in the North Harbour District) are on the Pacific Ocean, from Long Bay in the north to Devonport in the south.
These are almost all sandy beaches with safe swimming and most have shade from Norfolk pines.
Tamaki Drive beaches are located on the Waitemata Harbour in the upscale suburbs of Mission Bay and St Heliers in central Auckland.
These beaches are sometimes crowded with families on holiday and lined with a variety of shops. Swimming is safe.
The main local beer is "Number One". It is not a complex beer, but it is good and refreshing. Another local beer is called "Manta".
There is a lot of French wine available, but New Zealand and Australian wines may be better suited to the long journey.
The local tap water is perfectly safe to drink, but if you are concerned, bottled water is easily available.
The concept of flat white coffee is still somewhat alien to Australians. Small cups of black coffee are "espresso", cappuccinos have a lot of cream (not foam) and tea is served without milk.
Hot chocolate is up to Belgian standards. Juices are expensive but good.
Many people visit the Great Barrier Reef throughout the year and injuries from any of the above activities are rare on the Great Barrier Reef.
Nonetheless, heed the advice of authorities, obey all signs and pay close attention to safety warnings.
Box jellyfish are present near beaches and river mouths around Townsville from October to April each year, and occasionally at other times.
Sharks do exist, but they rarely attack people. Most sharks are afraid of humans and will swim away.
The main habitat of the saltwater crocodile is not the ocean, but the estuaries north of Rockhampton.
Booking in advance gives the traveller the peace of mind that there will be somewhere to stay when they arrive at their destination.
While travel agents may help you book other forms of accommodation such as camping, they usually work with specific hotels.
Travel agents often offer packages that include breakfast, airport transfers, and sometimes even flights and hotel accommodation.
They can hold the reservation for you if you need time to consider the price or to obtain other documents for your destination (such as a visa).
If any changes need to be made or any requests are required, they should be made through the travel agent first, rather than directly to the hotel.
At some festivals, the majority of attendees choose to camp on site, and most consider it an essential part of the festival experience.
If you want to be close to the action, you need to arrive early and claim a campsite close to the stage.
Remember, while the music on the main stage may have finished, other parts of the festival may still be playing music late into the night.
Some festivals have special camping areas for families with children.
If crossing the North Baltic in winter, check the location of the cabin, as sailing on ice can cause very unpleasant noise to those most affected.
St. Petersburg cruises include sightseeing in the city. Cruise passengers are exempt from visa requirements (see terms and conditions).

They often provide special food, drinks, and entertainment to keep guests happy and in the casino.
Some venues offer free alcohol. However, being drunk impairs judgment and all good gamblers know the importance of staying sober.
Anyone driving in high latitudes or over mountain passes should be prepared for snow, ice, or freezing temperatures.
On ice and snow, friction is low and you cannot drive as you would on a smooth asphalt road.
In a blizzard, enough snow can fall in a very short time to trap you.
Falling snow, blowing snow, or condensation or ice on the windows may also reduce visibility.
On the other hand, winter weather is common in many countries and traffic is rarely disrupted year-round.
Safari is perhaps the most attractive tourist activity in Africa and the highlight of many visitors' trips.
In common usage, the word "safari" refers to a journey by land to see the amazing African wildlife, especially on the savannahs.
Some animals (such as elephants and giraffes) tend to come close to the car, and standard equipment will allow good viewing.
Lions, cheetahs and leopards can sometimes be shy, and you will see them better with binoculars.
Game drives (also called "bush walks", "trekking", or "hiking") are essentially walking safaris, which can last for a few hours or several days.
The Paralympics will take place from August 24 to September 5, 2021. Some events will be held in other parts of Japan.
Tokyo will be the only city in Asia to have hosted the Summer Olympics twice, the first time in 1964.

Cancellation policies vary, but as of the end of March, most COVID-related cancellation policies do not extend to July 2020, when the Olympics were scheduled to take place.

Ironing wet clothes can help them dry. Many hotels have irons and ironing boards available for loan, even if they are not in the room.
If you don't have an iron, or don't like ironed socks, you can try using a hair dryer (if available).
Be careful not to make the clothes too hot (which could cause shrinkage, or in extreme cases, burn them).
There are many ways to purify water, and some are more effective against specific contaminants.
In some areas boiling for one minute is enough, in others it takes several minutes.
Filters vary in their effectiveness, and if you have concerns about this, you should consider purchasing sealed bottles of water from a reputable company.
Travelers may encounter pests that are not common in their home country.
Pests can ruin food, cause irritant reactions, or in more serious cases, allergic reactions, spread venom, or spread infectious diseases.
Infectious diseases themselves, or dangerous animals that can kill humans by violence, are usually not considered pests.
Tax-free shopping is the opportunity to purchase goods free of tax at certain locations.
Travelers to countries with high taxes can sometimes save a lot of money, particularly on products such as alcohol and tobacco.
The most challenging driving conditions on the Buffalo-Pittsburgh Highway are between Point Marion and Fairmont, where the road frequently passes through remote, rural areas.
If you are not used to driving on country roads, keep your wits about you: there are many steep grades, narrow lanes, and sharp turns.
The posted speed limits are significantly lower than those on the sections before and after, commonly 35-40 mph (56-64 km/h), and the importance of observing them is greater than on other sections.
However, surprisingly, the cell phone service is much better than on many other stretches of the route (such as Pennsylvania Wilds).
German pastries are good, and in Bavaria they are more colorful and similar to those of their southern neighbor Austria.
Fruit pastries are common, and apples are used for pastries year-round, with cherries and plums available in summer.
Many German baked goods also feature almonds, hazelnuts and other nuts. Popular cakes often go well with strong coffee.
If you want a small, rich treat, try a Berliner, Pfannkuchen, or Krapfen, depending on the region.
A curry is a dish based on herbs and spices, served with meat or vegetables.
Curry dishes can be either "dry" or "wet", depending on the amount of water used.
In the inland areas of northern India and Pakistan, curries often include yogurt; in southern India and some other coastal areas of the subcontinent, coconut milk is often used.
With 17,000 islands, Indonesian cuisine is a general term covering a wide variety of regional cuisines across the country.
But without further qualifiers, the term often refers to food that is native to the central and eastern regions of the main island of Java.

Javanese food is now available throughout the archipelago, and it features many dishes with simple seasonings, with the main seasonings being peanuts, chili, sugar (especially Javanese coconut sugar), and various spices.
Stirrups are hung on either side of the saddle to support the rider's feet.
They provide the rider with better stability, but they can also be a safety hazard as the rider's feet can get stuck.
If a rider is thrown from a horse but has a foot caught in a stirrup, he or she could be dragged along as the horse runs. There are some precautions that can be taken to minimise this risk.
First, most riders wear boots with heels and smooth, relatively narrow soles.
Secondly, some saddles, especially English ones, have safety straps that will drop the stirrup leathers from the saddle if the rider falls backwards off the horse.
Cochamó Valley, Chile's premier climbing destination, known as the Yosemite of South America, features a variety of granite monoliths and cliffs.
The views from the peaks are stunning. Climbers from around the world are constantly carving out new routes on its endless walls of rock.
Downhill winter sports, including skiing and snowboarding, are popular sports in which skis or snowboards are attached to your feet and you slide down snow covered terrain.
